title: How Not to Die: Discover the Foods Scientifically Proven to Prevent and Reverse Disease
excerpt: The definitive popular guide to evidence-based plant-based nutrition, showing how a whole-food plant-based diet can prevent, treat, and reverse the fifteen leading causes of death. Features the Daily Dozen framework.
rationale: Root book #3 for nutrition-science. How Not to Die provides the comprehensive practical framework for evidence-based plant-based nutrition, complementing the investigative (Salt Sugar Fat) and biochemical (The Case Against Sugar) approaches with an actionable lifestyle prescription.
keyIdeas: The leading causes of death are largely preventable through dietary and lifestyle changes
A whole-food plant-based diet addresses multiple chronic diseases simultaneously
Nutritional science is systematically distorted by industry funding and publication bias
The same dietary pattern that prevents disease can often reverse it once established
The Daily Dozen checklist provides a simple framework for optimal nutrition
keyTakeaways: Heart disease can be reversed with a plant-based diet, as shown by Dr. Dean Ornish and Dr. Caldwell Esselstyn
Dietary fiber from whole plant foods is the single most underconsumed nutrient that protects against chronic disease
The five leading causes of death (heart disease, cancer, lung disease, stroke, dementia) share common dietary roots
Animal protein consumption is linked to elevated IGF-1 levels, which promote cancer growth
Leafy greens and berries are the most nutrient-dense foods for disease prevention
whoShouldRead: Anyone concerned about preventing or reversing chronic disease
Patients recently diagnosed with heart disease, diabetes, or hypertension
Health professionals seeking an evidence-based nutritional framework
Readers looking for a comprehensive, actionable dietary guide
whoShouldSkip: Those committed to an omnivorous diet who do not want to examine evidence for plant-based eating
Readers who prefer moderate, flexitarian approaches over a dietary prescription
People looking for quick fixes rather than comprehensive lifestyle change
Skeptics who distrust systematic reviews of nutritional research
